explicit_text: I left from Louisville, Kentucky when I was inducted into the Army. That’s where I went for my induction process before heading to basic training. Louisville was the starting point of my military journey, and I remember them telling me they’d see me in a month or so. It was a big moment leaving home from there.
implicit_text: I left from the largest city in Kentucky when I was inducted into the Army. That’s where I went for my induction process before heading to basic training. That city was the starting point of my military journey, and I remember them telling me they’d see me in a month or so. It was a big moment leaving home from there.

explicit_text: I had hoped to join the United States Air Force because I thought their training would be more applicable to civilian life. An Air Force doctor even examined my knees and asked why I was in the Army, suggesting I might have had better luck getting into the Air Force. But ultimately, I ended up in the Army and not the Air Force. I also knew a guy from Newburgh, Indiana who had been in the Air Force before joining the Army and volunteering for Vietnam.

But ultimately, I ended up in the Army and not that other branch. I also knew a guy from Newburgh, Indiana who had been in that air service before joining the Army and volunteering for Vietnam.

explicit_text: I took my basic training at Fort Knox, which was a mix of National Guard and regular Army trainees. After basic, I stayed at Fort Knox for reconnaissance scout training, which was very tough and demanding. Fort Knox was where I learned vital skills like map reading, camouflage, and artillery adjustment. It was a key place in my military preparation before going overseas.

After basic, I stayed there for reconnaissance scout training, which was very tough and demanding. That post was where I learned vital skills like map reading, camouflage, and artillery adjustment. It was a key place in my military preparation before going overseas.

explicit_text: I was originally scheduled to go to Fort Bliss, Texas, for missile school after basic training. However, during training, I got a foot infection from the rain and couldn’t transfer until my profile expired. Eventually, my orders to Fort Bliss were pulled, and I stayed at Fort Knox instead for reconnaissance scout training. So, I never actually went to Fort Bliss.

explicit_text: I remember a guy from Newburgh, Indiana, who had previously served in the Air Force and then joined the Army. He volunteered to go to Vietnam but was instead sent to Germany, which I thought was ironic. He was one of the few people I knew personally who had a strong desire to serve in Vietnam. Newburgh was mentioned in connection with his military path.

That Indiana town was mentioned in connection with his military path.

explicit_text: My father was a World War II veteran who landed at Omaha Beach on D-Day. He didn’t talk much about his experiences, but I learned a lot from my mom and from Time Life books on World War II that I got after I married. My dad would sometimes point out his unit insignia in photos and tell me about being in Luxembourg and Germany. His service shaped some of my views on military duty.
implicit_text: My father was a veteran of the global conflict from 1939 to 1945 who landed at Omaha Beach on D-Day. He didn’t talk much about his experiences, but I learned a lot from my mom and from a well-known series of history books on that war that I got after I married. My dad would sometimes point out his unit insignia in photos and tell me about being in Luxembourg and Germany. His service shaped some of my views on military duty.

explicit_text: Omaha Beach was where my father landed during World War II on D-Day. He carried shrapnel from that battle and had firsthand recollections, though he rarely spoke about it. I only really understood the significance of Omaha Beach because of a schoolteacher who also participated in D-Day and would talk about it at school assemblies. Omaha Beach was a key part of my dad’s military story.

explicit_text: D-Day was a significant event in my father’s life during World War II when he landed at Omaha Beach. He was on the third wave, and a schoolteacher who spoke at our school was on the seventeenth wave, which created some tension. My father didn’t glorify his experience but it was clear it affected him deeply. D-Day was a defining moment in his military service and influenced my understanding of war.

It was a piece of his military journey I came to appreciate later.

explicit_text: I was drafted during the Vietnam War and eventually sent there after my training. The war wasn’t openly discussed much at my school or in my community, though there were some casualties and a few people I knew who served. I trained as a reconnaissance scout and went through Vietnam-specific training before deployment. My time in Vietnam was a defining part of my military service and life experience.

explicit_text: At Fort Knox, I went through reconnaissance scout training, which was very demanding and covered a wide range of skills like camouflage, explosives, map reading, and artillery adjustment. The training emphasized stealth and intelligence gathering rather than direct fighting. We had to complete a tough final test involving a timed ten-mile run with various tasks. This training prepared me for the complex missions I would face in Vietnam.

explicit_text: I flew out of Oakland, California when I left the United States for Vietnam. It was my first experience at O'Hare Airport, and luckily my mother’s second cousin was on the same flight, which helped me navigate the airport. We barely made our connecting flight, which was a bit stressful. Oakland was the departure point for my overseas deployment.
